title: vLLM cannot serve quantized MoE beyond VRAM on consumer cards - Ollama is the OMEN engine
rule: On VRAM-constrained consumer GPUs (OMEN RTX 5070 12GB), engine choice for beyond-VRAM models is llama.cpp-family (Ollama), NOT vLLM: vLLM 0.24 UVA weight-offload crashes on quantized MoE (Marlin kernel: b_scales is not on GPU). Do not retry vLLM-with-offload for MoE AWQ/GPTQ until the kernel gap is fixed upstream or the one open rung is tested.
detail: Measured 2026-07-02: qwen3-coder:30b (30B-A3B MoE, 18GB) on Ollama, GPU+RAM split on OMEN: 54.6 tok/s generation, ~60 tok/s prompt eval, 16s load. vLLM 0.24 same model class (QuantTrio AWQ): crash loop x12 - Marlin MoE GEMM requires quantization scales on-GPU, UVA offloader leaves them host-resident. --enforce-eager does not help (kernel-level check).

Open rung (untested, blocked on Derek sudo): --quantization moe_wna16 routes through triton kernels which MAY tolerate UVA host memory; fails earlier in fresh WSL for want of a C compiler. Test after: sudo apt install build-essential.

Thesis relevance: strong artifact for the Windows/consumer-GPU story - the batching-throughput king cannot play at all when weights exceed VRAM; graceful CPU spill is the whole game on consumer cards, and llama.cpp-family owns it.

Ops notes: one 12GB GPU = ONE 30B engine at a time (Ollama warm uses ~11GB; vLLM startup needs its allocation free) - engine A/Bs on OMEN are sequential windows, not concurrent. WSL idles the distro down when no interactive session persists, killing user-systemd services mid-load - keep-alive required (folded into the login-start tunnel script).
alternative: Alternatives considered: smaller model fully in VRAM on vLLM (dirty A/B - different model); vLLM CPU backend (not competitive); dual-GPU (hardware change, out of scope).
